title: Getting Copies of Military Service Records
summary: Service records are often needed to claim veterans benefits, and getting copies is more straightforward than it might seem.
category: After a Loss II: Notifications & Accounts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Getting Copies of Military Service Records | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Service records are often needed to claim veterans benefits, and getting copies is more straightforward than it might seem.
whatYouNeedToKnow: The DD-214 is the key discharge document most benefits require.
Copies can be requested through the National Archives if the original is lost.
Processing can take time, so requesting early is worth it.

paragraphs: The DD-214 (Certificate of Release or Discharge from Active Duty) is the document most VA benefit applications require — if the family already has a copy, that's the fastest path; if not, it can be requested through the National Archives' National Personnel Records Center.
Because processing these requests can take weeks, requesting a copy as early as possible — even before all benefit paperwork is ready — helps avoid delays down the line.

nextSteps: Check whether the family already has a copy of the DD-214.
If not, request one through the National Archives promptly.
Keep the copy with other estate documents once received.
disclaimer: General information — the National Archives can confirm current request procedures and timelines.
